content.text: Triage Cadence and Ownership Rules

Bug triage is not a single event — it is a recurring process operating at multiple timescales simultaneously. P0 bugs require real-time response; P3 bugs are reviewed at a weekly cadence. Without an explicit cadence structure, the natural tendency is to triage reactively: engineers only look at the bug queue when a customer escalates, which means the queue is always behind and priorities drift. A structured cadence with defined owners at each tier ensures the bug queue reflects reality, not accumulated neglect.

attrs.alt: Triage cadence diagram showing real-time, daily, weekly, and quarterly cycles with owners and activities

content.text: Real-Time Triage: P0 and P1

P0 and P1 bugs enter a real-time triage workflow that begins the moment the bug is filed. Real-time triage does not wait for the next scheduled meeting. The on-call engineer and the engineering manager are the two roles responsible for real-time triage. When a P0 is filed, the on-call engineer must assign an owner within 15 minutes and confirm that the first fix attempt is underway within one hour. If the on-call engineer cannot assign an owner because everyone is occupied, the EM is notified immediately.

The speed requirement for P0 triage is not bureaucratic formalism — it is the mechanism that prevents scope creep. A P0 bug that is not owned within 15 minutes of filing is a P0 bug where the scope estimate was made by whoever filed it, with no engineering validation. Scope estimates from support agents without log access are frequently wrong. The first act of the assigned engineer is to validate the scope, not to start fixing.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from enum import Enum

class TriageState(str, Enum):
    FILED         = "filed"          # just created, no owner
    OWNER_ASSIGNED = "owner_assigned" # engineer claimed it
    SCOPE_CONFIRMED = "scope_confirmed" # engineer validated scope from logs
    FIX_IN_PROGRESS = "fix_in_progress"
    RESOLVED      = "resolved"
    WONT_FIX      = "wont_fix"

@dataclass
class P0TriageTracker:
    bug_id: str
    filed_at: datetime
    state: TriageState = TriageState.FILED
    owner: str | None = None
    owner_assigned_at: datetime | None = None
    scope_confirmed_at: datetime | None = None
    fix_started_at: datetime | None = None

    OWNER_ASSIGNMENT_SLA_MIN = 15
    FIRST_FIX_ATTEMPT_SLA_MIN = 60

    def assign(self, engineer: str, at: datetime | None = None) -> None:
        self.owner = engineer
        self.owner_assigned_at = at or datetime.utcnow()
        self.state = TriageState.OWNER_ASSIGNED

    def is_owner_assignment_breached(self, as_of: datetime | None = None) -> bool:
        ref = as_of or datetime.utcnow()
        if self.owner_assigned_at is not None:
            return False
        elapsed = (ref - self.filed_at).total_seconds() / 60
        return elapsed > self.OWNER_ASSIGNMENT_SLA_MIN

    def is_fix_attempt_breached(self, as_of: datetime | None = None) -> bool:
        ref = as_of or datetime.utcnow()
        if self.fix_started_at is not None:
            return False
        elapsed = (ref - self.filed_at).total_seconds() / 60
        return elapsed > self.FIRST_FIX_ATTEMPT_SLA_MIN

    def alerts(self, as_of: datetime | None = None) -> list[str]:
        msgs = []
        if self.is_owner_assignment_breached(as_of):
            msgs.append(f"{self.bug_id}: owner assignment SLA breached — page EM")
        if self.is_fix_attempt_breached(as_of):
            msgs.append(f"{self.bug_id}: first fix attempt SLA breached — page VP Eng")
        return msgs
Daily Triage: P2 Review

P2 bugs are reviewed in a 15-minute daily triage standup. The meeting has a fixed agenda: review all P2 bugs filed since the previous standup, validate or update scope estimates, decide whether any P2 should be promoted to P1 based on new log data, and confirm that bugs already in "fix in progress" state are actually being worked. The attendees are the support engineering manager and the current on-call engineer. No product managers or external stakeholders attend — the daily triage is an operational meeting, not a prioritisation debate.

The most important activity in the daily P2 review is scope validation. A P2 bug filed from a support ticket typically has a scope estimate based on customer reports, not log evidence. By the time of the daily review, the on-call engineer should have pulled log data to validate whether the scope is "single customer" or something wider. A P2 bug that log data reveals affects 5,000 users per day is promoted to P1 at the daily review — it does not wait for the weekly full triage.
Weekly Triage: Full Team Review

The weekly triage meeting runs for 30 minutes and covers the full bug queue. All engineers attend. The agenda covers three topics: newly filed bugs that have not been through daily triage (typically P3 candidates), bugs that are blocked on external dependencies or on decisions that require group input, and bugs that have been in the queue for more than two sprint cycles without a fix. The weekly triage is the venue for P3 classification — bugs are assessed for severity, scope, and feasibility, and assigned a P3 owner or returned for more information.

The weekly meeting is deliberately short. When triage meetings run long, the recurring cost of attendance rises, attendance drops, and engineers route around the meeting by filing bugs directly into the backlog without triage. 30 minutes is achievable only if the pre-work is done: the EM prepares the agenda the day before by pulling the bug queue, identifying which items need group input, and ordering them by urgency. Items that do not need group input are handled before the meeting.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class WeeklyTriageAgenda:
    week_of: date
    prepared_by: str

    new_bugs_for_classification: list[str]      # bug IDs; needs priority and owner
    blocked_bugs: list[tuple[str, str]]          # (bug_id, blocker description)
    aging_bugs: list[tuple[str, int]]            # (bug_id, days_open); > 2 sprints

    def time_budget_minutes(self) -> dict:
        # 30-minute total budget
        new_count = len(self.new_bugs_for_classification)
        blocked_count = len(self.blocked_bugs)
        aging_count = len(self.aging_bugs)
        # Approximate: 2 min per new bug, 3 min per blocked, 1 min per aging
        new_min = min(new_count * 2, 15)
        blocked_min = min(blocked_count * 3, 10)
        aging_min = min(aging_count * 1, 5)
        total = new_min + blocked_min + aging_min
        if total > 30:
            # Trim aging review — it is least urgent
            aging_min = max(0, 30 - new_min - blocked_min)
        return {
            "new_classification_min": new_min,
            "blocked_review_min": blocked_min,
            "aging_review_min": aging_min,
        }

    def is_overloaded(self) -> bool:
        budget = self.time_budget_minutes()
        return sum(budget.values()) >= 28  # approaching 30-min limit
Ownership Rules and the Single-Owner Principle

Every bug must have exactly one owner at all times. Shared ownership is no ownership — when two engineers are both listed as owners of a bug, neither one treats it as their responsibility to ensure it gets fixed. The single-owner principle applies from the moment of P0/P1 assignment through to resolution. For P2 and P3 bugs, ownership is assigned at the daily or weekly triage meeting.

Owner responsibilities are specific: the owner confirms the scope estimate with log data, drives the fix to completion or hands it off with an explicit transfer note, updates the bug status at least every 48 hours for P1 and every week for P2, and escalates if they are blocked. "I'm waiting on the model team" is a valid state, but it must be recorded in the bug ticket as a blocker with a named contact, not silently held. A bug with no update for seven days triggers an automated staleness flag regardless of its stated status.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

@dataclass
class BugOwnership:
    bug_id: str
    priority: str
    current_owner: str
    owner_assigned_at: datetime
    last_update_at: datetime
    status: str
    blocker: str | None = None
    blocker_contact: str | None = None

    # Max gap between updates before staleness flag fires
    STALENESS_THRESHOLD_HOURS = {
        "P0": 2,
        "P1": 12,
        "P2": 48,
        "P3": 168,  # 7 days
    }

    def is_stale(self, as_of: datetime | None = None) -> bool:
        ref = as_of or datetime.utcnow()
        threshold = self.STALENESS_THRESHOLD_HOURS.get(self.priority, 168)
        hours_since_update = (ref - self.last_update_at).total_seconds() / 3600
        return hours_since_update > threshold

    def transfer(self, new_owner: str, transfer_note: str, at: datetime | None = None) -> None:
        if not transfer_note:
            raise ValueError("transfer_note is required for ownership transfers")
        self.current_owner = new_owner
        self.owner_assigned_at = at or datetime.utcnow()
        self.last_update_at = self.owner_assigned_at

    def set_blocker(self, description: str, contact: str) -> None:
        self.blocker = description
        self.blocker_contact = contact
        self.status = "blocked"
        self.last_update_at = datetime.utcnow()
Cross-Team Ownership for AI-Specific Bugs

AI-specific bugs frequently cross team boundaries. A model quality regression may be diagnosed by the support engineering team, rooted in model training decisions owned by the ML team, and fixed by the model serving team. In this situation, ownership must still reside in a single person — but ownership means coordination responsibility, not technical fix authority. The owner drives the bug to resolution by coordinating across teams, tracking the fix, and ensuring no team treats the handoff as permanent.

The owner rotation for cross-team bugs follows a defined protocol: the team that discovers the bug owns it through diagnosis. When diagnosis identifies that the root cause is in another team's domain, ownership transfers to that team's on-call engineer with an explicit transfer note that includes the reproduction case and the current diagnosis. The originating team's owner transitions to a secondary-contact role and is responsible for verifying the fix from the customer perspective when it ships.
Bug Category
Initial Owner
Transfers to
Fix verification
Model quality regression
Support engineering (L3)
ML Platform team
Support engineering runs eval
API latency regression
Support engineering (L3)
Model serving team
Support engineering checks customer SLA
Safety violation in output
Support engineering (L3)
Policy team (first); ML if model fix needed
Policy team confirms fix + monitors
SDK / API contract bug
Support engineering (L2)
Developer experience team
Support engineering confirms customer unblocked
Triage Meeting Anti-Patterns
Using triage as a design review:

When a bug reveals a systemic architecture problem, the triage meeting is not the place to design the fix. Triage decides priority and owner; fix design happens in a separate session. Triage meetings that expand into design reviews overrun their time budget and train engineers to skip attendance.
Assigning bugs to teams instead of people:

"Assigned to: Model Serving Team" is no assignment. A team cannot be held accountable for a delivery; an individual can. Triage must always result in a named individual as owner, even for bugs that will require team effort to fix.
Re-triaging bugs every week without resolution:

A bug that appears on the weekly triage agenda three weeks in a row without progressing is not a triage problem — it is a prioritisation or blocking problem. After two weeks without progress, the EM must investigate the block and either resolve it or close the bug as won't fix.
Allowing customers to set priority by escalation:

When a customer escalates loudly, the commercial relationship manager may pressure engineering to raise a bug's priority outside the triage process. Priority changes made this way are undocumented, create inconsistency, and establish an incentive for all customers to escalate everything. All priority changes must go through the triage process or the EM override procedure with a written rationale.
Quarterly Backlog Hygiene

The quarterly backlog hygiene session operates on P3 bugs that have not been touched in 90 days. The objective is to ensure the backlog is a prioritized, actionable list rather than a graveyard of stale issues. Every P3 bug older than 90 days without an update is reviewed and classified into one of three outcomes: close as won't fix, promote to P2 (if product context has changed), or retain at P3 with a written rationale explaining why it remains relevant.

The hygiene session is run by the engineering manager with the technical leads from each team. It takes approximately 60 minutes for a backlog of up to 100 P3 bugs. For teams with larger backlogs, the session is split into two 45-minute sessions by bug category. After the session, any bugs retained at P3 have their "last reviewed" date updated, which resets the 90-day decay clock. Bugs that are closed must have a close reason ("won't fix — model version deprecated", "won't fix — customer churned", "closed — duplicate of #1234") rather than a silent closure.
Tooling Requirements for the Triage System

The triage cadence only works if the tooling supports it. Engineers need to be able to filter the bug queue by priority, scope, and last-update date in under 30 seconds. If finding the P2 bugs that have not been updated in 48 hours requires a custom query that the EM has to rebuild each day, the daily triage becomes a chore that gets skipped. The bug tracking system must support at minimum: priority labels, scope labels, owner fields, last-update timestamps, and automatic staleness flags.

For AI-specific bugs, the tracking system should also support model version fields and quality metric fields. A query like "all P2 bugs affecting claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 where quality_delta_pct < -10" should be answerable in one click. Without this, the team cannot assess whether a model version deprecation will close a batch of bugs or whether a quality regression is an isolated incident or part of a pattern.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Protocol

class BugQueryFilter:
    priority: list[str] | None = None          # e.g. ["P0", "P1"]
    scope: list[str] | None = None
    owner: str | None = None
    stale_after_hours: float | None = None     # bugs with no update in N hours
    model_version: str | None = None           # AI-specific field
    quality_delta_lt: float | None = None      # e.g. -10 → bugs where quality dropped > 10%
    filed_after: str | None = None             # ISO date
    filed_before: str | None = None

class BugTracker(Protocol):
    def query(self, filter: BugQueryFilter) -> list[dict]:
        """Returns matching bug records sorted by priority then filed_at.""\"

    def update_status(self, bug_id: str, status: str, note: str) -> None: ...
    def assign_owner(self, bug_id: str, owner: str, note: str) -> None: ...
    def close(self, bug_id: str, reason: str) -> None: ...
    def get_staleness_report(self) -> dict:
        """Returns bugs exceeding their priority-specific staleness threshold."""
        ...

def daily_triage_prep(tracker: BugTracker) -> dict:
    """Pre-compute the daily standup agenda from live bug data."""
    return {
        "p2_no_scope_validation": tracker.query(BugQueryFilter(
            priority=["P2"],
            # log_validated_scope not yet set — proxy: status == "filed"
        )),
        "p2_stale": tracker.query(BugQueryFilter(
            priority=["P2"],
            stale_after_hours=48,
        )),
        "p2_blocked": tracker.query(BugQueryFilter(priority=["P2"])),  # filter for blocker field set
        "new_since_last_standup": tracker.query(BugQueryFilter(priority=["P2"])),  # filed in last 24h
    }
Triage During High-Incident Periods

During a P0 incident, the on-call engineer and EM are occupied with incident response. The daily triage standup and the weekly triage meeting are suspended for the duration of the incident. All new bugs filed during an active P0 incident are tagged as "filed during incident" and receive a mandatory post-incident review — many of them will be symptoms of the incident root cause and will be closed when the incident postmortem identifies the fix.

The suspension of normal triage during incidents is a deliberate trade-off: incident response requires focused attention, and splitting that attention between the incident and the bug queue produces worse outcomes on both fronts. However, the triage backlog that accumulates during an incident must be cleared within 48 hours of incident resolution. The EM is responsible for scheduling a catch-up triage session in that window. Incident periods are not an excuse for a permanently delayed queue — they are a defined pause with a required recovery.
Triage Metrics and Program Health

The triage program produces three health metrics that are reviewed monthly: mean time to owner assignment (MTTOA) by priority, escalation rate (bugs that move up in priority after initial triage), and closed-without-fix rate for P3 bugs. MTTOA measures whether the cadence is working — if P0 MTTOA exceeds 15 minutes consistently, the on-call rotation is under-staffed or the paging system is not reaching engineers. Escalation rate measures triage accuracy — if 30% of P3 bugs are promoted to P2 at the weekly review, the initial triage is systematically under-classifying scope.
Metric
Target
If Off Target
MTTOA (P0)
< 15 minutes
Review on-call coverage and paging reliability
MTTOA (P1)
< 2 hours
Review daily standup attendance and P1 queue size
P3 → P2 escalation rate
Review scope estimation process; update triage training
P3 closed without fix
40–60% (healthy)
< 40% = over-triaging to P3; > 60% = too many invalid bugs filed
Summary

Triage cadence operates at four timescales: real-time for P0/P1 (15-minute owner assignment, 1-hour fix attempt), daily for P2 (15-minute standup with log-based scope validation), weekly for all new bugs (30-minute full team), and quarterly for P3 backlog hygiene (60-minute review). Every bug has exactly one named owner at all times. Cross-team AI bugs transfer ownership with an explicit transfer note but retain a secondary contact from the originating team. Triage anti-patterns — design reviews in triage, team-level assignments, customer-driven priority overrides — undermine the system's ability to produce consistent, predictable scheduling signals.
